hooks.command: jq -n --arg f "$(git -C "$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR" diff --cached --name-only)" '{hookSpecificOutput:{hookEventName:"PreToolUse",additionalContext:("STAGED FILES about to be committed:\n" + (if $f == "" then "(none)" else $f end) + "\nA concurrent session shares this git index. If any staged file is NOT part of your change, do not commit the whole index — use explicit pathspecs: git commit -m ... -- <your-paths>")}}' || true

hooks.prompt:
A git commit is about to run: $ARGUMENTS

First look at the tool call's command above: if it does not actually execute `git commit`, reply allow immediately without running anything.

Review the STAGED changes before allowing it. Run `git diff --cached --stat`, then `git diff --cached`. Read surrounding code only if needed to judge.

Check against this repo's rules (CLAUDE.md, .claude/rules/):
1. Layer boundaries — lib/src/domain stays pure Dart (no Firebase imports); data owns Firebase details; application orchestrates; features is UI only. No direct Firestore .collection() calls in features/, no booking status transitions performed client-side.
2. Hygiene — no hardcoded secrets or API keys, no leftover debug prints, no large commented-out code blocks.
3. Regression bar — a bug fix must include (or demonstrably already have) a regression test; new or changed business logic in lib/src/{domain,data,application} should come with tests in the same commit.

DENY only on clear violations: a committed secret, a broken layer boundary, client-side booking status mutation, a bug fix with no regression test, or new critical-layer logic with zero tests. Otherwise ALLOW — for style nits or when uncertain, allow and briefly mention the concern.

A git commit is about to run: $ARGUMENTS

Scope check first: if the command does not actually execute `git commit`, reply allow immediately. Then run `git diff --cached --name-only`; if NO staged file is under lib/src/features/ or lib/src/app/, reply allow immediately — this review only applies to UI changes.

Otherwise you are the UI/UX lead for Outalma. Read .claude/agents/ux-guardian.md (your rubric) and .claude/rules/design-bar.md, then review ONLY the staged UI changes: `git diff --cached -- lib/src/features lib/src/app`. Judge from the perspective of a first-time smartphone user in Dakar who may not read well: understandable in 3 seconds, icons over text, one dominant action.

DENY only on clear violations INTRODUCED by this diff: new async UI without a loading and error state; a new list/grid without an empty state; hardcoded colors or text styles where theme tokens (context.oc / Theme) exist; touch targets clearly below 48dp; user-facing strings hardcoded when the screen already uses l10n; color-only information with no icon/text pairing. When denying, name the exact file and what to change.

For subjective or pre-existing issues, ALLOW and append at most 3 short 'UX notes' bullets with the most valuable improvements.

A firebase deploy is about to run: $ARGUMENTS

If the command does not actually execute `firebase deploy`, allow immediately. Otherwise you are the deployment guardian for Outalma — security is deny-by-default (.claude/rules/firebase-safety.md) and booking transitions are server-authoritative. Determine the targets from --only (absent = everything deploys).

IF RULES deploy (firestore, storage, or no --only): run `git diff HEAD -- firebase/firestore.rules firebase/storage.rules` to see uncommitted edits about to go live, then Read firebase/firestore.rules and firebase/storage.rules. DENY on clear weakening: any `allow read` or `allow write: if true` on real collections; blanket auth-only writes to sensitive collections (users, bookings, chats, reviews); client-side writes to booking `status` or chat creation (must stay Cloud-Functions-only); removal of owner/participant checks. Deploying UNCOMMITTED rules edits is suspicious — deny unless the diff is plainly a tightening.

IF FUNCTIONS deploy: run `cd functions && npm run build`; DENY if tsc fails. If the working tree has uncommitted functions/src changes, warn that untested code is being deployed and deny unless the change is trivial.

When denying, name the exact rule/file and why. For anything ambiguous but not clearly weakening, allow with a one-line warning.
